Their colleague, also from Partners in Health, who contracted Ebola in Sierra Leone, is being treated at NIH and is in fair condition. The CDC expects all 17 monitored patients to be released by Thursday. The Ebola outbreak in West Africa has claimed over 10,000 lives since December 2013. This article discusses several real-life survival stories of individuals who were stranded at sea for extended periods. Louis Jordan survived after his sailboat capsized, relying on ingenuity and resourcefulness. Jose Salvador Alvarenga spent over a year adrift in the Pacific Ocean, surviving on rainwater, his own urine, and sea turtles. Ron Ingraham endured 12 days at sea after his sailboat was damaged by a storm. Adrian Vasquez survived 26 days in the Pacific after his boat's engine failed, while Hiromitsu Shinkawa was swept out to sea during the 2011 Japanese tsunami and survived by waving a red flag. These stories highlight the resilience and determination required to survive such harrowing experiences.

A fire broke out at a Russian shipyard where a nuclear submarine was being repaired. The incident occurred in the Zvyozdochka shipyard in northwestern Russia, involving a submarine that had been under repair since November 2013. The fire started due to welding work and was contained without any injuries or damage to the reactor or other hazardous materials on board. The crew evacuated safely, and the fire posed no threat to people or the facility.

Jia Jiang, a Chinese-American entrepreneur, embarked on a 100-day project called "100 Days of Rejection" to confront his fear of rejection after losing investment in his tech startup. His experiences, documented in his book "Rejection Proof," helped him overcome his fears and inspired others to do the same. Through various challenges, such as asking for an impossible donut order, Jiang demonstrated that rejection is often more about the rejector than the rejectee and can be turned into opportunities. His project aimed to demystify rejection and encourage people to embrace it as a way to grow and achieve their goals. Jiang shares eight tips for handling rejection effectively, emphasizing the importance of perseverance, confidence, and understanding that rejection is often subjective.

Six young men from Minnesota, aged 19 to 21, were arrested for planning to join ISIS in Syria. The group of friends, who had been plotting for 10 months, were caught after driving to San Diego with plans to cross into Mexico. This case highlights the issue of peer-to-peer recruiting within the Somali immigrant community in Minnesota, as the group recruited each other rather than being influenced by external recruiters. Author Peter Gallagher has filed a lawsuit against Joss Whedon, Drew Goddard, Lionsgate, and Mutant Enemy, claiming they stole the idea for "The Cabin in the Woods" from his 2006 novel "The Little White Trip: A Night In the Pines." Both works involve groups of young people terrorized by monsters in a horror-film scenario orchestrated by unseen forces. Gallagher seeks $10 million in damages, citing similarities in plot elements, character names, and scenes. The case highlights the ongoing debate over originality in the film industry.

The name of Palestinian teenager Mohammed Abu Khdeir, who was killed by three Israelis in July, was mistakenly included on an Israeli memorial wall honoring fallen soldiers and victims of terrorism. His family and an organization representing terror victims in Israel have objected to his inclusion, arguing that his death was not a legitimate act of terrorism. As a result, the National Insurance Institute of Israel has agreed to remove Abu Khdeir's name from the memorial wall following complaints from his father and the organization. The case is currently being heard in court against the three suspects involved in his killing.

A Japanese court has dismissed a petition by residents to delay the reactivation of nuclear reactors at the Sendai plant in Kagoshima, ruling that new safety standards are valid. This decision could support the government's plans to restart reactors, which have been offline since the 2011 Fukushima disaster. Meanwhile, another court had previously halted the restart of reactors at the Takahama plant due to safety concerns, but the Sendai ruling may embolden the government to push forward with its nuclear energy strategy despite public opposition. The government aims to reduce reliance on expensive fossil fuels and boost the economy through nuclear power, though renewable energy sources like solar and hydro are also being considered as alternatives.

A Consumer Reports study analyzed award-seat availability for major U.S. airlines based on 70 million passenger trips over two years. Southwest Airlines topped the list with the most award tickets (11.9 million) and the highest percentage (11.5%) of award seats, thanks to its unique policies and extensive route network. JetBlue ranked last with the fewest award tickets (892,000) and lowest percentage (4.5%) of award seats due to limited routes and frequent expiration of miles. Customer satisfaction scores were highest for Southwest and JetBlue, while American and United faced criticism for high fees. Consumers should consider both award-seat availability and overall satisfaction when choosing an airline for award redemption.

Ben Affleck requested that a PBS show, "Finding Your Roots," remove details about his great-great-great grandfather who owned slaves from an episode about his family history. The show's producer, Harvard professor Henry Louis Gates Jr., agreed to omit the information, as revealed in hacked emails. While Gates maintains editorial control, Affleck's request was likely influential. Both parties are commended for handling the situation responsibly, as the show is an entertainment program rather than an investigative news one. Affleck now regrets making the request, but had the right to do so given the sensitive nature of the information. The incident highlights the balance between transparency and privacy in media portrayals of public figures.

The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) called for a 24-hour ceasefire in Yemen to prevent further casualties, especially in Aden, as the situation worsens due to ongoing airstrikes and ground fighting. The UN Security Council discussed the conflict but did not reach a decision, with Russia submitting a draft resolution that lacked key elements. The conflict began after Houthi rebels, a minority Shiite group, forced President Hadi from power, leading to Saudi-led airstrikes against the rebels. At least 519 people have been killed and 1,700 wounded in the past two weeks, with tens of thousands fleeing to neighboring countries. Brynn Duncan, a 21-year-old from Easley, South Carolina, suffers from severe and unpredictable food allergies due to mast cell disease, which affects her immune system. Her condition requires constant monitoring and treatment, including multiple emergency room visits and hospitalizations. Despite the challenges, Brynn remains resilient, describing her daily crises as part of her routine. Her mother, Melissa, administers medication through a chest port to help manage her daughter's symptoms, which can be triggered by various substances and lead to severe reactions. The family's journey to find a diagnosis involved numerous specialists and eventually led them to Dr. Lawrence Afrin, who confirmed their suspicion of mast cell activation syndrome.

Maksims Uvarenko, the Latvian goalkeeper for CSKA Sofia, has not been paid for three months due to the club's financial troubles. Despite this, he appreciates the passionate support from the club's unique fans. CSKA Sofia faces significant debt issues and may need emergency financial backing from fans to avoid expulsion from the Bulgarian top division. The club, which has a rich history including reaching European semi-finals, currently struggles with financial problems stemming from past large transfers and wages. The team is second in the standings, trailing current champions Ludogorets. CSKA's next match against Ludogorets could result in bonuses for the players if they win.

Tottenham defender Ben Davies will face former Wales teammate Sam Vokes in a crucial Barclays Premier League match against Burnley. Davies, who acknowledges the personal connection, emphasizes the importance of focusing on the game and not letting sentimentality affect the match. Both teams are under pressure; Tottenham aims to secure European qualification, while Burnley fights for survival. Tottenham's squad returns from international duty largely fit and in good form, with manager Mauricio Pochettino optimistic about their chances. The match takes place at Burnley's Turf Moor stadium.

In Bhiwandi, a suburb of Mumbai, a seven-year-old boy was raped and severely burned by two youths after being lured to an isolated area to buy food. The boy escaped with 30% burns and is receiving treatment. Police have registered cases under the Indian Penal Code and Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act and are investigating the incident. The location is Padma Nagar, and authorities are working to identify and apprehend the suspects.

Harvey Boulter, a pro-Ukip businessman who forced Liam Fox to resign as Defence Secretary, is funding campaigns against two Conservative candidates, Tom Tugendhat and Fox, in their respective constituencies. This move is seen as revenge for a libel case where Fox won damages against Boulter, with Fox's father being the judge in the case. Boulter has donated £30,000 each to the Ukip candidates in Somerset and Kent, while also spending another £30,000 to try to defeat Tugendhat.

At the Indianapolis Zoo, a group of meerkats have been practicing basketball on a makeshift hoop installed in their enclosure, entertaining visitors with their inquisitive and comical attempts. The zoo also hosted various March Madness-themed activities during the weekend. Tonight, the NCAA championship game between Wisconsin Badgers and Duke Blue Devils will take place at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis. Key entities include meerkats, Indianapolis Zoo, Wisconsin Badgers, Duke Blue Devils, and Lucas Oil Stadium.

During a three-day family holiday in Tenerife, Loushanna Craig had her biometric ID card stolen, which prevented her from flying home on Ryanair due to concerns about her legal status. Despite having proper documentation, including a 'right to remain' stamp and a visa, she was detained and faced difficulties returning to the UK. After intervention from a community activist, she eventually returned home, but the incident left her feeling humiliated and vowed never to use Ryanair again. The Home Office and Ryanair maintained that the biometric residence permit cannot be used as a passport substitute for international travel.

Sam Tomkins, a 26-year-old England full-back, is considering returning to Wigan after feeling homesick during his time with the New Zealand Warriors. Wigan has an option to bring him back, and Tomkins acknowledges that Wigan would be a nice choice, but he remains open to other possibilities. Tomkins left Wigan in 2014 and played for the Warriors until now, where he recently signed a three-year contract but is now leaving due to missing his family and friends in England. The Warriors have replaced Tomkins with Roger Tuivasa-Sheck.

Raheem Sterling and Theo Walcott, both English wingers, are set to face each other at Arsenal's Emirates Stadium. Sterling, 20, has been Liverpool's standout player this season, contributing significantly in goals and assists, while Walcott, 26, has struggled with injuries and inconsistent performances. Despite their contrasting career trajectories, both are currently negotiating new contracts with their clubs, and there is speculation about a potential transfer between them. Sterling's future remains bright due to his youth and potential, whereas Walcott's career has shown signs of underachievement despite early promise.

Yorkshire's Tim Bresnan hopes a strong start to the domestic season will bolster his case for an England Test recall. Raymond Billam, a 40-year-old grandfather, was found guilty of benefit fraud after secretly filming showed him playing football for a pub team despite claiming severe disabilities. Billam received a 26-week suspended prison sentence, 200 hours of unpaid work, and a four-month curfew. The court also noted his participation in multiple Sunday League matches where he demonstrated full physical activity inconsistent with his claimed disabilities.

The incoming chief executive of Rotherham council is likely to receive a higher salary of up to £200,000, compared to the previous £160,000 earned by Martin Kimber, due to the negative publicity surrounding the child grooming scandal. This increase is partly attributed to the need to attract experienced candidates amid the council's poor performance highlighted in a government report. The council's HR chief justified the higher pay as necessary given the challenging circumstances. The decision has raised concerns among some councillors.

Liverpool midfielder Philippe Coutinho believes winning the FA Cup would salvage their season, given their slim chances of finishing in the top four. Following recent losses to Manchester United and Arsenal, Liverpool now trail fourth-place Manchester City by seven points with seven games remaining. Despite acknowledging the difficulty of achieving their primary objective of Champions League qualification, Coutinho remains optimistic about securing silverware as a consolation prize. Liverpool will face Blackburn in their FA Cup quarter-final replay on Wednesday night.

Arsenal have had a strong run in the Premier League, defeating Liverpool convincingly and showing potential as title contenders. Deanna Robinson, a 38-week pregnant Iraq war veteran, claims she was hit multiple times by a deputy during an arrest at her parents' home. The case may be included in a new judge-led inquiry into historical child abuse involving public bodies. MI5 has faced longstanding accusations of both protecting child abusers and failing to act against prominent figures involved in crimes.

In southern Germany, the Georgiritt Easter Monday horseback parade, dating back to the 18th century, involves locals in traditional costumes riding through the snow to receive blessings. In Slovakia and other Central European countries, Wet Monday (Śmigus-Dyngus) traditions include men dousing women with water, followed by women giving men colorful eggs. These rituals, which may have pagan origins, are nearly extinct, with only a few villages continuing these customs. In Hungary, the Vízbeveto tradition includes men sprinkling water on women, who then give them colored eggs, often involving folk dancers. In China, a panda named Lu Lu set a new record for the longest recorded mating session between pandas, lasting 7 minutes and 45 seconds with his partner Zhen Zhen. This achievement earned him the nickname "The Enduring Brother" and made him a sensation in China. Lu Lu's performance surpasses the average duration of lovemaking for both American men and pandas, highlighting the importance of understanding these animals' mating behaviors for successful breeding programs. The Sichuan Giant Panda Research Centre closely monitors such encounters to enhance reproduction efforts among pandas. Summary: A panda named Lu Lu set a new record for the longest recorded mating session with his partner Zhen Zhen, lasting 7 minutes and 45 seconds. This achievement earned Lu Lu the nickname "The Enduring Brother"

Michelle Schwab, an assistant director at a KinderCare childcare center in Columbus, Ohio, has been charged with child endangerment for allegedly dropping her two-year-old son into a cheetah pit at Cleveland Metroparks Zoo. The child suffered minor injuries but the animals did not approach him. Schwab, who has three sons and a degree in therapeutic childcare, has taken a leave of absence from her job and faces up to six months in jail and a $1,000 fine. The zoo has also launched its own investigation into the incident.

John Travolta has spoken out against the controversial Scientology documentary Going Clear, stating that his 40-year experience with the organization has been positive and that he would not watch the film. The documentary alleges that Scientology uses blackmail and physical abuse to keep members, including Travolta, loyal. The Church of Scientology denies these claims, calling the documentary a "bigoted propaganda piece." Travolta refuses to address the allegations directly, maintaining that discussing them would be "a crime" to him. The film also claims that Scientology leaders have used various forms of punishment, including a "prison camp," to manipulate members and prevent them from leaving. The Church of Scientology has launched a counter-campaign to discredit the documentary.

Up to 2500 passengers on the Carnival Spirit cruise ship are stranded outside Sydney Harbour due to severe weather conditions, including eleven-metre waves. The ship has experienced some minor damage, but all passengers and crew remain safe. The ship cannot dock until the weather improves, which may take up to 48 hours, as it is mandatory for a harbor pilot to board the ship before entry. Passengers are being kept occupied with on-board activities, but conditions are uncomfortable, with some experiencing significant motion sickness. The ship's departure schedule has been altered, and passengers are advised to expect further delays.

In the winter of 2014-15, Boston experienced an unprecedented amount of snow, breaking numerous records. After the snow melted, large piles remained, filled with trash and debris. Despite the unsanitary conditions, one skier filmed himself skiing down a makeshift slope made from the leftover snow, which contained items such as broken glass and trash. Boston's total snowfall for that season reached 108.6 inches, surpassing the previous record by over a foot.

Australian plus-size model Laura Wells, who has achieved international success as a size 14 model, has revealed that her thinner colleagues often resort to extreme measures such as eating only one cracker and a couple of glasses of water daily before fashion weeks. This practice, according to Wells, is unhealthy and takes a toll on both physical and mental health. Wells, who initially resisted becoming a plus-size model due to her misunderstanding of the term, now advocates for more acceptance of diverse body types in the fashion industry. She believes that the industry should celebrate all body shapes rather than striving for unrealistic standards.

Christine Davidson, a 61-year-old woman from Adelaide, South Australia, passed away after battling terminal brain cancer for 14 years. Her diamond engagement ring was allegedly forcefully removed and stolen from her finger by a thief at a nursing home just days before her death. The family has launched an online appeal to recover the ring, which held significant sentimental value. They have received widespread support, with over 117,000 shares and nearly 17,000 likes on a Facebook post. The police have been informed, and a local jewellery store is offering a $1000 reward for information leading to the ring's recovery.

Rising ocean temperatures due to climate change threaten the traditional British dish of fish and chips. As waters warm, popular fish like haddock and plaice may migrate to cooler areas, reducing their availability and potentially increasing prices. Researchers from the University of Exeter and the University of Bristol developed a model using long-term fisheries data and climate projections to predict that fish distributions will shift, with cold-water species facing challenges and warmer-water species becoming more prevalent. This shift could lead to changes in the types of fish available for fishing in the UK, necessitating a move towards warmer-water species like red mullet and John dory.

Sarah Vine, a British journalist, argues that she should be considered for the position of Jeremy Clarkson's replacement on the BBC's Top Gear show. She emphasizes her passion for cars, drawing parallels between her own experiences and Clarkson's, while highlighting the show's significant female viewership. Vine also addresses potential gender biases, referencing criticism faced by Sue Perkins when suggested for the role. Her letter concludes with a detailed description of her own automotive expertise and enthusiasm.

Mohammed Suleman Khan, a 43-year-old tax fraudster, was sentenced to four years in prison for defrauding the UK government of £450,000 over nine years. He now faces an additional ten years in prison unless he pays back £2.2 million within six months. Khan built a lavish £2.3 million mansion in Pakistan, modeled after Buckingham Palace, using hidden wealth, while living modestly in the UK. Known as "The General" in criminal circles, Khan's case shocked Birmingham's criminal underworld despite his mysterious and mythical status among them. Khan was also briefly under suspicion in connection with the unsolved murder of Tony Yaqub in 2004.

An analysis of Hillary Clinton's Twitter followers reveals that only about 44% are genuine users, with at least 15% being fake accounts, compared to just 4% for President Obama. This comes amid other concerns about Clinton's social media presence, including reports that over 46,000 of her Facebook 'likes' might be purchased rather than organic. These findings add to the Clinton campaign's embarrassment and raise questions about the authenticity of her online support base. Additionally, a recent campaign video featuring a character portrayed as a grandmother was revealed to be a former abortion lobbyist. The article also mentions that Clinton is on a listening tour in Iowa, where she was reportedly not recognized by locals.

Researchers have demonstrated for the first time that higher doses of vitamin E, found in foods like kale and almonds, can help mitigate oxidative stress on immune cells, potentially aiding the body's response to infections. The study, conducted on mice, showed that a high dose of vitamin E protected T cells from oxidative damage, allowing them to multiply and effectively fight off viral infections. While the results are promising, further research is needed to determine the implications for human health and whether vitamin E supplements would be beneficial in everyday situations involving oxidative stress. The study was led by Manfred Kopf at ETH Zurich. Key points include the role of vitamin E in protecting T cells, the importance of oxidative stress in weakening the immune system, and the potential benefits of vitamin E supplementation during infections or other stressful conditions.

British Prime Minister David Cameron has admitted that his 11-year-old daughter Nancy compares him to Phil Dunphy, an embarrassing dad from the US sitcom Modern Family. Nancy often uses the phrase "That is so Phil Dunphy" to mock her father's attempts at being cool, as evidenced during a recent shopping trip where she criticized his fashion sense. Cameron has also revealed that Nancy plans to write a memoir about her time growing up in Number 10, including an incident where her father accidentally left her in a pub. The comparison highlights the gap between Cameron's efforts to present himself as a modern, relatable leader and how his children perceive him.

Jessica Bialek, a 37-year-old Sydney photographer, was found safe and returned to her home after going missing on Wednesday morning. Her husband, Sabino Matera, expressed his gratitude to the community and police for their assistance in locating her. The couple had recently returned from a trip to Melbourne, and Bialek was last seen leaving her home to walk to the bank. Police were initially involved due to the unusual nature of the disappearance. The local community played a significant role in the search effort, which led to Bialek's safe return.

In 2013, technology entrepreneur Elon Musk attempted to sell his struggling Tesla to Google for $6 billion, but the deal fell through when Tesla unexpectedly turned profitable in May of that year. Musk had proposed that Google buy Tesla and expand its factory, allowing him to run it as a subsidiary for eight years. Tesla's fortunes improved due to Musk's efforts to resolve technical issues with the Model S, leading to a surge in orders and a significant increase in stock value. Google later invested $1 billion in Musk's other venture, SpaceX. The article highlights the potential for major tech companies to collaborate or compete in the automotive industry. Musk's ambitious plans for Tesla and SpaceX continue to attract significant investment and attention from other tech giants.

According to a Suncorp Bank report, Australians have spent 50% more than planned on digital devices and services, totaling $20 billion or an average of $2,300 per person in the past year. Families with children are spending 50% more on digital connectivity compared to other household types. Mobile phone bills represent the largest expense, with families with children paying an additional $244. Over one-third of households do not budget for technology expenses or underestimate their costs significantly. Men spent twice as much as women on tech-related items. The report highlights the increasing importance of technology in daily life despite financial strain.

According to the article, eye contact is a powerful tool for signaling sexual interest, as it is the most effective way to do so and our eyes are highly sensitive and responsive. The piece outlines five techniques for using eye contact to attract someone: the four-and-a-half second scan, the slide and settle, the flirting triangle, the blink, and the lover's gaze. Each technique involves specific eye movements and durations designed to communicate attraction and interest. The article suggests that prolonged eye contact can trigger intense emotional responses, while certain patterns of eye movement can indicate varying levels of interest or arousal. To learn more about these techniques and other tips for being more attractive, readers are directed to visit traceycox.com.

Claudia Winkleman, 43, the current co-host of BBC's Strictly Come Dancing, has become the first presenter of the show to receive a Bafta nomination. Despite being absent for nearly a month due to her daughter's Halloween costume fire incident, Winkleman's nomination marks a significant achievement for the program, which has received a total of three nominations this year. The show continues to outperform ITV's X Factor and Britain's Got Talent, which have once again failed to secure any nominations. Winkleman competes in the entertainment performance category alongside Ant and Dec, Graham Norton, and Leigh Francis. The nominations were chosen by 4,000 Bafta members, and the show faces competition from other popular programs like The Great British Bake Off and Game of

A survey by YouNeedaBudget.com found that 64% of adults spend more when shopping with friends due to peer pressure and a desire to show off. Key overspent categories included food, clothing, and entertainment. Despite setting budgets, 95% still exceeded their limits, often citing sales as justification. To avoid overspending, experts recommend prioritizing spending and sticking to long-term financial goals.

TV star Karren Brady, alongside David Cameron, announced 16,000 new apprenticeships at a National Grid training center in Newark. The Conservative Party aims to create three million more apprenticeships by the next election, with support from companies like Costa Coffee, Morrisons, and National Grid. Despite her hopes for a more prominent role, Brady was relegated to helping Cameron with his jacket and holding it while he spoke. The initiative emphasizes the growing importance and respectability of apprenticeships in the UK job market.

Kyle Knox, a 23-year-old Londoner, was found dead near the foot of Ben Nevis three weeks after he went missing while attempting to climb the mountain. The severe weather conditions, including temperatures as low as -7°C and winds up to 70mph, made the search challenging. Knox, who lacked experience, was reportedly climbing alone without proper gear. The body was identified by the Royal Navy helicopter and his family was notified. Searches involved mountain rescue teams, a helicopter, and search dogs.

A terror alert was triggered on the Thames when a passer-by reported a suspicious missile on a barge in Reading. Thames Valley Police and an Army bomb disposal team investigated but found the "weapon" to be a non-threatening, 4-foot-long, ornamental missile. A local resident criticized the prank, stating it was inappropriate given current global security concerns. The barge was moored near a children's play area in Christchurch Meadows. The incident occurred near Windsor Castle, where the Queen was present. Pastor Edward Fride of Christ the King Catholic Church in Ann Arbor, Michigan, advised his parishioners to obtain concealed pistol licenses and carry guns, citing increased crime and reduced police presence as reasons. This recommendation came after Fride acknowledged that faith alone may not be sufficient protection. The church now offers eight-hour CPL classes, though Bishop Earl Boyea of the Diocese of Lansing opposes carrying guns in church premises and considers them gun-free zones. Fride's stance reflects a shift from his previous pacifism, emphasizing the need for self-defense in certain situations.

Audrey Pekin, a 19-year-old Australian, was raped twice by Henry Alafu on Christmas Eve in Bali. Alafu lured her to his remote home and then followed her into a taxi, where he allegedly attacked her again. Despite having Alafu's phone number, Facebook profile, and address, Indonesian police failed to apprehend him, allowing him to escape back to Nigeria. The Pekin family is frustrated with the lack of action and calls for better enforcement of laws against sexual predators in Bali. Interpol has now issued a red notice for Alafu's arrest. Dawn Williamson, a 39-year-old mother from Scotland with a 30-year phobia of snakes, was cured of her ophidiophobia during a live segment on ITV1's This Morning. Therapists Nik and Eva Speakman successfully treated Dawn in just ten minutes, using a method that involved proving to her that her fear was irrational. Dawn went from breaking down in tears at the sight of a plastic snake to confidently stroking a 9-foot Burmese python, marking the first time viewers witnessed the therapy process in action. The Speakmans have helped 60 others overcome their fears and phobias on live television.

Toshiba's humanoid robot, Aiko Chihira, is set to work at a Tokyo department store's information desk, offering six-minute guided tours and assistance in Japanese and sign language. This marks the first time the robot will provide customer service. Meanwhile, other innovative robots like the Murata Cheerleaders, which use gyroscopes and infrared sensors to perform synchronized dances without falling, are attracting attention at the Ceatec Japan electronics trade show. Toshiba aims to develop more social robots by 2020 for companionship and healthcare applications.

Actress Tori Spelling was hospitalized after suffering a severe burn on her right arm when she tripped and fell onto a hot grill at Benihana's Japanese restaurant during an Easter brunch with her family. The incident occurred in Encino, California, and required skin grafts at the Grossman Burn Center. Despite the pain, Spelling maintained a brave demeanor for her children, who were visibly worried. This latest setback comes amid a series of personal challenges for the 41-year-old actress, including marital issues and conflicts with her family. To date, she is recovering and hoping for a full recovery.

Paul Monk, a fugitive from Essex, was arrested by Spanish police at his villa in Javea, near Alicante, for questioning about the murder of Francis Brennan, a British victim whose body was found on a beach. The operation involved heavily armed police, air support, and the discovery of an imitation firearm with a silencer and nearly £100,000 in cash. Monk was also wanted by the Metropolitan Police for drug offenses and was part of the National Crime Agency's Operation Captura campaign. He is expected to face charges in Spain before potential extradition to the UK.

Raymond Lee Fryberg Jr., a father, pleaded not guilty to illegally purchasing a firearm that his 15-year-old son Jaylen used to kill four classmates and take his own life at Marysville Pilchuck High School. Fryberg had a permanent domestic violence protection order from a Tulalip Tribal Court that prohibited him from owning firearms, but he allegedly bought the gun without disclosing the order on the federal firearms purchasing form. The case highlights issues with the reporting of tribal court orders to state and federal databases, which may have prevented the background check from flagging the restriction. A federal grand jury indicted Fryberg on a single count of unlawful possession of a firearm by a prohibited person, setting his trial for June 22. Fryberg faces up to 10 years in prison and a

Sharon Winters, a 39-year-old woman with learning difficulties and depression, was murdered by Kevin Hawke, a man she met on an online dating site. Her brother, Stephen Robinson, has spoken out about the case, warning women to be cautious when dating online and encouraging them to seek help if they are in abusive relationships. Hawke, who had been drinking heavily, stabbed Winters to death after an argument over what to watch on television. The attack left Winters with over 90 injuries, including damage to her liver, spleen, lungs, and heart. Hawke was sentenced to 17 and a half years in prison.

Transavia France, a low-cost subsidiary of Air France-KLM, is promoting its flights by bundling them with snacks such as crisps, gummy bears, and cereal bars. Customers purchasing these items at participating Carrefour City shops, vending machines, or a cinema can find a voucher code redeemable for a discounted flight to Barcelona, Dublin, or Lisbon between June 1 and October 24. The promotion, called 'SnackHolidays,' was delayed due to a recent plane crash and offers minimal savings compared to regular fares. The campaign aims to attract customers through a unique and engaging promotional strategy.

Sir Ranulph Fiennes, a 71-year-old explorer, completed the Marathon des Sables in Morocco, becoming the oldest British participant to do so. Despite previous health issues including two heart attacks and a double bypass surgery, Fiennes managed to finish the six-day, 256km race, which took place in harsh desert conditions. He credited his success to determination and fundraising efforts for Marie Curie Cancer Care, of which he has raised nearly £1 million. Fiennes faced significant challenges during the fourth stage, where he ran for over 30 hours in extreme heat, but ultimately crossed the finish line with just 13 minutes to spare.

Liz Norden, the mother of two Boston Marathon bombing victims who lost their legs, opposes Mark Wahlberg's plan to turn the tragedy into a movie. Norden argues that the pain and recovery process for victims and their families are still ongoing, making it too soon for such a project. The article also mentions that Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, one of the bombers, was recently convicted of 30 charges related to the bombing, while his brother Tamerlan was killed during a police confrontation. Other Boston residents criticized Wahlberg for announcing the film during the trial, deeming it inappropriate. Wahlberg's representatives did not respond to requests for comment.

Poppy Moore, granddaughter of the late Bobby Moore, celebrated her wedding with an intimate ceremony at Chelsea Register Office in London, attended by 12 family members, followed by a reception for 50 guests at the Mayfair Hotel. The groom was Sam Myers, and the bride wore a white dress with floral details and carried a bouquet including a photo of her late father. Poppy, along with her siblings, commemorated the 20th anniversary of Bobby's death by participating in a West Ham United football match. Bobby Moore, the 1966 World Cup-winning captain, passed away from bowel cancer at age 51. His widow Tina and daughter Roberta were among the guests at Poppy's wedding.

Conservative Minister Esther McVey, who won her Wirral West seat in 2010 with a narrow majority, faces intense criticism and personal attacks ahead of the upcoming election. She has called on Ed Miliband to condemn the 'scurrilous' campaign against her, which includes graffiti calling her a 'murderer' and a song mocking her. Despite the attacks, McVey remains defiant and highlights the support she receives from other female cabinet members.

At Toronto's Pearson International Airport, a man believed to be mentally disturbed was Tasered after refusing to comply with security staff and attempting to enter a restricted area. The incident, captured on video, shows the man mumbling and holding a black briefcase when he was hit by a Taser. He was later transferred to a hospital under Canada's Mental Health Act and may not face criminal charges. Authorities considered the safety of all involved and the man's potential capabilities and intentions. The man, in his 20s, was not seriously injured.

He was also a respected art collector and was often seen wearing a diamond-studded watch. A 19-year-old model named Chanel from California is featured in an episode of MTV's True Life, where she discusses her extreme methods to maintain her youthful appearance, including bathing in pig's blood. Despite being a vegetarian, Chanel believes this practice, which she claims dates back thousands of years, keeps her skin soft and tight. Her family is skeptical, and while she initially plans to continue, she later states she has no desire to repeat the experience. The article also mentions historical and modern examples of blood usage in beauty treatments, such as the legend of Countess Elisabeth Bathory and Kim Kardashian's "vampire facial." The effectiveness of these practices remains unproven.

Liverpool manager Brendan Rodgers has been fined for leaving a rented property in Accrington in a state of disrepair for three years. The house, which was purchased by Rodgers and his business partner Judith O'Hagan in 2007, was found to be in poor condition with broken windows, doors, and rubbish in the garden. The pair were found guilty of ignoring an improvement notice from Hyndburn Council and were ordered to pay £775 in fines and costs. This conviction comes amidst Liverpool's ongoing struggles, including their recent FA Cup exit. Rodgers still has a contract with Liverpool until 2018.

Porsche conducted a world-first stunt in the Australian Outback, showcasing their $900,000 918 Spyder supercar by driving it at 350km/h on an unrestricted stretch of road. The car, featuring a petrol-electric hybrid system, was piloted by Craig Baird, a professional race car driver. This promotional event is part of a tour to highlight Porsche's E-hybrid models, with the 918 Spyder being one of 918 units produced in 2015. The company plans to use this event to attract attention to their hybrid technology and limited-edition sports car.

ESPN's Britt McHenry, a 28-year-old sports reporter from New Jersey, was suspended for one week after a video surfaced showing her verbally attacking a tow truck company employee in Arlington, Virginia. The incident occurred when McHenry, who had her car towed from a Chinese restaurant parking lot, unleashed a series of insults regarding the employee's appearance, education, and job. Despite being warned that she was being filmed, McHenry continued her offensive rant, leading to her suspension and potential further disciplinary action from ESPN. McHenry later apologized, acknowledging that her actions were "insulting and regrettable." Advanced Towing has not commented on the matter.

Maison Souquet, a newly opened five-star hotel in Paris, offers a unique blend of opulence and edginess inspired by the Belle Epoque era. Located in the trendy Pigalle district near the Moulin Rouge, the hotel features luxurious rooms designed by Jacques Garcia, with opulent gold accents and plush velvet furnishings. The Celestial Spa, complete with a shimmering star ceiling, is a standout feature, while the hotel also provides a range of amenities such as Hermes bath products and private dining services. The hotel caters to couples and groups seeking a romantic and indulgent experience in a historically significant area of Paris.

Model and chef Chrissy Teigen posted a photo of her stretch marks on Instagram, inspiring hundreds of women to share their own photos using the hashtag #loveyourlines. The campaign aims to promote body positivity and encourage women to embrace their natural marks, which can result from weight gain or pregnancy. The love your lines movement was started by two anonymous east-coast mothers who hoped to improve female body image. Since Teigen's post, many women have shared pictures of their stretch marks, including those from pregnancy and other life changes.

In a tragic incident of road rage, 36-year-old St. Charles Parish Sheriff's Deputy Burt Hazeltine was shot three times while directing traffic in a school zone near New Orleans. The suspect, 56-year-old Paul Devillier, a former Transportation Safety Administration worker, was arrested after a brief struggle with police. Hazeltine, though critically injured, remained in stable but guarded condition at the hospital. The shooting stemmed from a verbal altercation between the two men over traffic control. Devillier, who claimed to be an NCIS agent during the incident, faces serious charges. Hazeltine, a 15-year veteran of the force, is survived by his wife and four children.

Yaakov Naumi, a 32-year-old photojournalist from an ultra-orthodox Jewish family in Israel, has captured a series of intriguing rituals and customs practiced by his community. His photographs include a man lying in an open grave to prolong life, a chicken being swung to transfer sins, and a dance during a protest against mandatory military service. Naumi aims to showcase the essence of these rituals rather than portraying the ultra-orthodox as odd, and his work highlights the unique practices and traditions of this community. The images provide insight into the rich cultural tapestry of ultra-orthodox Judaism.

Former NHS head Sir David Nicholson has criticized Labour for not committing to an additional £8 billion annually for the health service, calling for a political consensus on the issue. He warns of a "substantial financial problem" facing the NHS and accuses politicians of ignoring the issue to focus on election chances. Health Secretary Jeremy Hunt and Deputy Prime Minister Nick Clegg have supported the need for the extra funding, while Labour leader Ed Miliband remains opposed to it.

During a press conference, Josephine Witt, an activist formerly associated with Femen, threw confetti at European Central Bank President Mario Draghi, yelling "End the ECB dictatorship!" before being removed by security. The incident occurred due to the ECB's perceived enforcement of austerity measures and lack of gender diversity. Despite the disruption, Draghi maintained composure and continued the press conference, reaffirming the ECB's commitment to its bond-buying stimulus program until September 2016 or until inflation targets are met. The event garnered significant media attention, with #Draghi trending on Twitter. The ECB is investigating the incident. In Grafton, a woman was seen wielding two dildos during a street altercation outside the Roches Family Hotel late Friday night. Eyewitness Nick Weller described how the woman threatened bystanders with the sex toys while a fight involving at least five girls ensued. Grafton Police attended but no formal complaints were filed. The hotel management disputed the incident, stating it was merely a heated discussion that quickly resolved. No one reported the woman's unusual behavior to authorities.

A study reveals that British homeowners are overvaluing their properties by an average of £74,000, leading buyers to negotiate aggressively. This misperception persists despite a cooling housing market, with sellers clinging to the belief that they can achieve peak prices. As a result, buyers are increasingly treating asking prices as starting points for negotiations, and some estate agents are overvaluing properties to secure listings, knowing they'll need to reduce prices later. The imbalance between asking and sold prices highlights the disconnect between market realities and seller expectations.

PayPal's top developer, Jonathan Leblanc, discussed plans to develop edible and injectable passwords during a presentation titled 'Kill All Passwords'. These devices would be powered by the body and could include sensors to monitor health metrics before transmitting encrypted data. However, PayPal clarified that these ideas were speculative and not part of current development plans. The company aims to improve user security through emerging biometric technologies like vein recognition and heartbeat bands, while also emphasizing its role in the FIDO alliance and fingerprint scanning innovations.

Sophie English, an Australian adoptee born in Vietnam during the Vietnam War, embarked on a journey to reconnect with her "motherland" after decades of searching for answers about her birth mother. Accompanied by ABC reporter Sally Sara, she traveled to Vietnam, where she met another adoptee, Le My Huong, who had found her mother and returned to Vietnam. This encounter provided a profound emotional experience for both women, allowing them to share their stories and offer support. Although the trip did not guarantee closure, it offered valuable insights and hope for English to move forward. The documentary, Foreign Correspondent, documented this emotional journey.

Ed Miliband criticized David Cameron and other EU leaders for ceasing search and rescue operations in the Mediterranean, which led to hundreds of deaths among refugees attempting to reach Europe. Miliband urged the UK to take a "fair share" of refugees fleeing conflicts in the Middle East and North Africa. The debate intensified following reports of 950 people drowning in a single incident, prompting an emergency EU summit to address the migrant crisis. Meanwhile, officials debated whether to focus on stopping people from boarding boats or targeting human traffickers.

The former brothel, known as Club 417, where Wei Tang was convicted of keeping five Thai women as sex slaves, is now for sale in Melbourne for around $1.3 million. The Edwardian-style property, located on Brunswick Street in Fitzroy, features six bedrooms, a lounge with a bar, and a sitting room. Tang was the first person in Australia to be found guilty under sexual servitude laws in 2008, after which the brothel ceased operations in 2013. The property is being marketed for various uses, including a business, bar, or restaurant.

Researchers plan to drill into the Chicxulub crater in Mexico to investigate the extinction of dinosaurs 66 million years ago. Recent studies suggest that the asteroid impact did not cause a global firestorm due to insufficient heat near the impact site to ignite plants. Instead, the heat pulse was too brief and localized. The drilling project aims to provide more detailed evidence about the impact's effects and its role in the mass extinction event.

The family of 4-year-old Austin West, who drowned in a pool in Gastonia, North Carolina, discovered that scammers had set up multiple fake GoFundMe pages in his name before they could create their own. The Wests are now trying to raise $10,000 for funeral costs through a legitimate GoFundMe page and an additional campaign selling T-shirts on Booster.com. As of the report, only $315 had been raised through the GoFundMe page. The incident highlights the vulnerability of families in crisis to online fraud.

On April 22, 1915, during World War I, German forces used chlorine gas for the first time in warfare, releasing it over Flanders Fields from their positions, causing terror and agony among French troops. This marked a significant shift in combat tactics, leading to the use of various gases by both sides throughout the conflict, with over 1 million soldiers exposed and 90,000 killed. The event also spurred the creation of more advanced gas masks and eventually led to the Geneva Protocol banning chemical weapons in 1925. French commander Georges Lamour, who witnessed the first gas attack, was never heard from again, leaving his wife to believe he was still alive for years.

A restaurant called The Steer Inn in Wilberfoss, East Yorkshire, received severe penalties after a health inspector discovered mouldy food and other hygiene violations following a customer complaint about dog feces on the floor. The owner, David Crossfield, admitted to 17 charges related to food safety breaches and was fined £15,262, including costs and surcharges. The inspection revealed multiple issues such as expired food being served, cross-contamination risks, and inadequate cleaning practices. Crossfield, who took over the business without proper food safety knowledge, expressed regret and stated the restaurant had closed down. The East Riding of Yorkshire Council emphasized their efforts to improve food safety standards through guidance, but the owner's negligence led to these harsh penalties.

Cleveland police officer Michael Brelo is charged with voluntary manslaughter for killing two unarmed suspects, Timothy Russell and Malissa Williams, during a high-speed chase in November 2012. Brelo, the only officer charged, allegedly stood on the hood of the suspects' car and fired 15 rounds after other officers had stopped shooting. Prosecutors argue that Brelo violated departmental policies and acted out of rage, while his defense claims the officers were justified in fearing for their lives. The case highlights issues of police conduct and excessive force in Cleveland, leading to a federal investigation and potential reforms.

Former Metropolitan Police Commissioner Lord Stevens is facing an investigation by the Independent Police Complaints Commission (IPCC) into allegations that he covered up police corruption during the Stephen Lawrence murder investigation. This probe stems from a complaint by Stephen's father, Neville Lawrence, regarding a 1998 letter from Lord Stevens stating that no officers involved in the case were under corruption investigations. The Metropolitan Police referred the matter to the IPCC after receiving a public complaint, and the investigation follows the findings of the Ellison review, which suggested that relevant material related to corruption might have been destroyed or lost.

In 2012, four young sisters from Italy were forcibly returned to their father after being taken to Australia by their mother, who claimed the father was abusive. The girls, now aged 12 to 17, are now living in Italy and appear to be settled, contrary to their hysterical behavior when first returned. Their mother, Laura Garrett, has claimed the girls were unhappy in Italy, but the girls themselves now seem content and are attending school and dating. The situation highlights the complexities of international custody disputes and the challenges faced by children caught in such conflicts.

A 24-year-old man named Tavon Watson, who received a birthday gift of a $220,000 Lamborghini, tragically killed his 36-year-old passenger Gary Terry, an operations manager at the Richard Petty Driving Experience, when the car crashed into a guardrail at Walt Disney World in Florida. The accident occurred during a driving lesson, and while Watson was hospitalized, Terry was pronounced dead at the scene. The Exotic Driving Experience, where the incident took place, offers high-speed driving experiences in luxury cars and is operated by Petty Holdings, which plans to close the Orlando location due to unrelated reasons.

Matthew Kenney, a 34-year-old Florida man, became erratic after smoking Flakka, a potent and dangerous designer drug. He ran naked through traffic in Fort Lauderdale to escape imaginary killers who he thought had stolen his clothes and wanted to kill him. Kenney has a history of arrests and was hospitalized for a psychiatric evaluation. Flakka, which can cause hallucinations, psychosis, and superhuman strength, has seen a significant increase in usage in Florida, with over 670 incidents reported in 2014 compared to just 85 in 2012.

An Icelandair flight from Reykjavik to Denver was struck by lightning shortly after takeoff, causing a hole in the plane's nose. Despite the damage, the Boeing 757 completed the 3,740-mile journey to Denver without incident, and no one was injured. While passengers initially did not notice the damage, it became apparent upon landing as a gaping hole was discovered. The airline stated that the pilots followed protocol and the plane landed safely, with the damaged aircraft now under inspection and repair.

Key points:
- Icelandair flight 671 from Reykjavik to Denver
- Lightning strike caused a hole in the plane's nose
- Plane completed the journey safely to Denver
- No injuries reported
- Aircraft is being inspected and repaired
- Investigation

The Conservative Party included in its manifesto plans to extend the right-to-buy policy to 1.3 million social tenants, a move criticized by housing associations who oppose the idea and threatened to challenge it legally. Additionally, the article highlights the high salaries of housing association chief executives, such as Jane Ashcroft earning £332,000 annually, drawing criticism from opposition parties and suggesting the policy may face legal challenges due to potential breaches of charity law and human rights.

Sarah Stage, a 30-year-old lingerie model from Los Angeles, became an internet sensation after sharing sexy pregnancy selfies. Four days after giving birth to her son James Hunter, she posted a photo of her toned post-baby body in 'granny panties', gaining praise from her 1.6 million Instagram followers. Her son weighed 8 pounds and 7 ounces at birth, dispelling concerns about her trim figure during pregnancy. Sarah, known for her fitness routine and healthy lifestyle, continues to share photos of her post-birth recovery and her newborn son.

A new North Korean school textbook claims that Kim Jong-un learned to drive at age three and won a yacht race at nine, part of efforts to indoctrinate younger generations. The controversial claims, contained in "Kim Jong Un's Revolutionary Activities," detail his early talents and are part of North Korea's propaganda to present a positive image of the country. These accounts are being taught alongside other subjects to students, despite their questionable veracity. The textbook is one of several ways the regime attempts to maintain control and glorify the Kim dynasty.

Boris Johnson discusses the resurgence of the British automotive industry, highlighting its growth and success in recent years. He contrasts this progress with the Labour Party's proposed policies, which he believes would revert the country to the economic struggles of the 1970s. Johnson argues that the current Conservative government's approach, led by David Cameron, is essential for maintaining and building upon the economic recovery and continued success of British manufacturing. He emphasizes the importance of supporting a capitalist and market-driven economy to ensure future prosperity.

Lisa Skinner, a 52-year-old science teacher, shot and wounded her estranged husband Bradley Skinner, 59, after he broke into their home in South Huntsville, Alabama, armed with a pistol and a knife. The incident occurred despite a restraining order against him. Police arrived and, after Lisa refused to drop her shotgun, shot her, leaving her hospitalized. The couple had been estranged for nearly 11 years, with Lisa fearing for her life due to her husband's violent behavior and drug use. The husband was previously charged with harassing communications and domestic violence. Lisa had also sought multiple protective orders against him. The case highlights the dangers faced by victims of domestic violence.

The Check It gang, comprising over 200 gay and transgender youths from Washington D.C., forms the subject of a new documentary. Despite facing violence, discrimination, and poverty, the gang members are working to break the cycle of violence through fashion initiatives. The film, directed by Dana Flor and Toby Oppenheimer, highlights their struggles and dreams, aiming to raise awareness about their plight and capabilities. The documentary has successfully reached its funding goal and will continue to address issues of HIV, anti-gay discrimination, and poverty.

British Airways has introduced a Mindfulness Programme called "Mindfulness for Travel" to enhance the experience of long-haul flights, particularly on its new Airbus A380 service between London and San Francisco. Developed in collaboration with mindfulness expert Mark Coleman, the programme includes meditation techniques and advice on healthy flying habits. These resources aim to promote relaxation and positivity among passengers, regardless of their seating class. The Mindfulness for Travel series consists of videos designed for various stages of the flight, along with tips on comfortable clothing, light meals, and gentle exercise to help travelers cope with jet lag. The programme seeks to make air travel more enjoyable and less stressful for all passengers.

Comcast and Time Warner Cable are set to meet with US Department of Justice officials next Wednesday to address competition concerns regarding their proposed $45 billion merger. Staffers at the Justice Department and Federal Communications Commission are worried about the combined company's potential dominance in the Internet broadband market. This meeting is the first between the companies and regulators since announcing the deal a year ago, and it may indicate that the agencies have not yet made a final decision on the merger. The companies hope to negotiate concessions that could satisfy the regulators' concerns. The stock prices of both companies dropped after news of the planned meeting emerged. The meeting could be the first of many, and it remains unclear if the companies can offer sufficient concessions to satisfy the regulators.

Kim Kardashian and husband Kanye West, along with their daughter North West, visited the Saint James Cathedral in Jerusalem for North's Armenian Orthodox baptism on Monday. The family received VIP treatment during the ceremony and were accompanied by security. The visit also included a surprise free concert by Kanye in Yerevan, Armenia, where the family traced their roots, and a documentary shoot about the Armenian Genocide. The family is expected to leave Israel on Tuesday after their two-day private visit.

Peter Mutty, a 57-year-old Australian grandfather, was sentenced to six months in jail and 75 lashes in Saudi Arabia for possessing alcohol, which is illegal there. Despite the Australian embassy's assistance, Mutty faces a travel ban preventing him from leaving the country. He has criticized the embassy for not taking his case seriously and lacking proactive action. After going public with his story, Mutty is now receiving more support from the embassy to lift the travel ban. Mutty expressed willingness to return to Saudi Arabia to work. The embassy stated they have been providing consular assistance to Mutty throughout his ordeal.

The community isolates itself from the outside world and is run by a group of elders. Elijah Overcomer, 26, left the commune with his wife Rosanna, 29, and their children in March 2013, comparing life in Gloriavale to the film The Village. They were motivated to leave to spend more time with their children and escape the strict rules and isolation of the community. Following their departure, the family received significant support from local churches and have since settled in Timaru.

A Shiba Inu named Shiba has turned a small tobacco shop in Tokyo into a popular tourist destination. The dog greets customers by opening a sliding window with its nose and paw, and often naps or eats cucumbers on the job. Since going viral on YouTube, the shop has seen an increase in visitors, including international tourists, who come to interact with Shiba and purchase items. The owner, Hisako Suzuki, reports that business has improved significantly due to the dog's internet fame. To preserve the unique experience, visitors are encouraged to sign a guestbook. The article highlights how Shiba's charm has transformed the shop into a beloved local attraction.

The upcoming Netflix series "Our Planet," a sequel to the acclaimed "Planet Earth," aims to showcase Earth's last wilderness areas using advanced digital camera technology. Produced by Silverback Films in collaboration with World Wildlife Fund, the eight-part series will premiere in 2019 after a four-year production process. The project seeks to highlight the beauty and importance of the natural world while emphasizing the urgency of global conservation efforts.

Ukip leader Nigel Farage visited an Army veterans' centre in Kent and called on Conservative voters to support him tactically to prevent a Labour government. He dropped his demand for David Cameron to resign before any post-election deal and hinted at potential cooperation. Farage's party has narrowed its target seats and emphasized its commitment to an early EU referendum. During the launch of Ukip's manifesto, the party faced criticism over diversity, with only one black person featured in the 76-page document. The manifesto also promised cuts to immigration, increased defense spending, and a significant tax reduction.

A 13-year-old boy from Barcelona, Spain, who had a 'hit list' of 25 names, carried out a shooting and stabbing attack at his school, killing a male teacher and wounding four others, including a female teacher and her daughter. The boy, identified by initials M.P.C., reportedly used a crossbow and a knife in the attack, which took place on the 16th anniversary of the Columbine High School massacre. He was arrested and is undergoing a psychiatric examination due to his age, as Spain does not consider children under 14 criminally responsible. The attack occurred in the Sant Andreu neighborhood of Barcelona, and the boy's sister fainted upon learning of the incident. A 39-year-old man, Jondrew Lachaux, allegedly impregnated his 17-year-old stepdaughter, who then gave birth alone at home while caring for her three-year-old sister, who later died. Lachaux and his wife, Kellie Phillips, left the teen and her sister behind on a road trip, returning only after the birth. The teen faced challenges in caring for both the newborn and her sister, leading to the latter's death due to malnutrition. Lachaux faces multiple charges, including child abuse and destroying evidence, while Phillips and the teen also face charges. The case raises questions about the appropriate response for the 17-year-old's actions.

Interior designer Abigail Ahern, known for her colorful approach to design, has released a new book titled "Colour" aimed at spreading her vibrant aesthetic. Ahern emphasizes the transformative power of color in home decor and provides ten tips for achieving color confidence, encouraging readers to embrace bold hues and experiment with unexpected color combinations. Her book features inspirational photographs from homes around the world, showcasing her virtuosic talent with color. Ahern advocates for ditching neutral tones and suggests layering textures instead to enhance a space without overwhelming it.

An upset Japanese woman took revenge on her cheating boyfriend by submerging his Apple devices, including an iMac, iPhone, and iPad, in a bathtub. The boyfriend later shared photos of the water-damaged gadgets on Twitter, receiving a mixed response from users. While some sympathized with the boyfriend, many praised the woman's actions, with one user comparing it to the famous Lorena Bobbitt case. The incident sparked discussions about the severity of the revenge and the consequences of infidelity.

Cengiz Güven, a 30-year-old Turkish security guard, oversees site security at Lone Pine, a hilltop battlefield cemetery in Gallipoli, where the second Australian dawn service will take place on Anzac Day. Güven, who previously provided security for celebrities such as Russell Crowe, Naomi Campbell, and Steve Tyler, remains confident despite concerns about security due to recent terror arrests in Australia and instability in Syria. With nearly 4000 national and paramilitary police and at least 1000 Turkish soldiers also on duty, the area will be heavily secured for the 100th anniversary of the Gallipoli landings. Martin Springall, a father from Toronto, shared a photograph of his daughter in Japan that featured mysterious black boots behind her. The image gained significant attention on Reddit, sparking debates about its authenticity and origin. Springall insists the photo was not altered and maintains there was no one else present during the shoot. Despite skepticism, he remains open to the possibility of a paranormal explanation. The photograph continues to intrigue many due to the unexplained appearance of the boots in the image.

Lord Janner, a former Labour MP accused of child abuse, transferred the deeds of his £2 million flat to his children in March 2015, shortly before the police raided his office and home. This move makes the flat difficult for potential victims to sue for compensation. Despite being deemed unfit to stand trial due to dementia, Janner was able to sign the transfer documents. Legal experts question whether he had the mental capacity to make such decisions, raising concerns about possible asset protection. The transfer occurred during ongoing civil cases seeking compensation from Janner for alleged abuse.

In Marana, Arizona, police officer Michael Rapiejko used his patrol car to run over armed robbery suspect Mario Valencia after Valencia allegedly fired a shot into the air and walked towards other officers. Valencia was hospitalized but survived the incident. The police chief defended Rapiejko's actions, stating that deadly force was necessary due to Valencia's possession of a loaded weapon and the potential danger to civilians. Valencia faces multiple felony charges, while Rapiejko was cleared of wrongdoing and returned to duty. The incident sparked debate about the use of deadly force by law enforcement.

A 50-foot sperm whale washed up on Pacifica State Beach in California and was found with graffiti spelling out the name of a local motorcycle gang, 'East Bay Rats Motorcycle Club.' The whale's body was still visible on the beach as of Wednesday, though the name had been washed away by the tide. An autopsy is pending to determine the cause of death, which currently shows no signs of blunt force trauma or broken bones. The motorcycle club did not respond to requests for comment. The whale may be burned or dragged out to sea after the investigation. A beachgoer noted the strong odor emanating from the decomposing carcass.

Until recently, it was impossible to distinguish between identical twins using DNA profiling due to their shared 100% genetic makeup. However, researchers at the University of Huddersfield have developed a new technique called high resolution melt curve analysis (HRMA) that can identify subtle differences in the number of hydrogen bonds within DNA, allowing for differentiation between twins. This breakthrough could help resolve cases where identical twins are suspects, such as the 2013 rape case involving Mohammed and Aftab Asghar, and potentially prevent wrongful convictions. The method relies on environmental factors that alter DNA methylation, which in turn affects the melting temperature of the DNA. While the technique shows promise, it has some limitations, including requiring a larger DNA sample and being less effective on twins raised in very similar

Kate Temple, a 33-year-old hairdresser, was jailed for 20 months after admitting to biting off part of Shelley Williams' ear during an argument at a charity event in County Durham. The incident occurred after Williams accused Temple of having an affair with her partner, leading to a scuffle where Temple bit off a chunk of Williams' ear. Temple's actions resulted in Williams needing reconstructive surgery and suffering long-term psychological effects. The judge acknowledged Temple's remorse but deemed the crime serious enough to warrant immediate imprisonment.

Robert Downey Jr abruptly left a TV interview with Channel 4 in London when the host asked about his past drug abuse and prison time, despite initially being cooperative. The actor became visibly uncomfortable and walked out when the conversation turned personal, leaving the interviewer Krishnan Guru-Murthy visibly embarrassed. Downey Jr has struggled with drug addiction and spent time in prison between 1996 and 2001, but has since maintained sobriety and built a successful acting career, particularly through his role as Iron Man.

Heida Reed, co-star of the BBC1 series Poldark, criticizes the intense focus on Aidan Turner's physique as sexist and potentially detrimental to the show's reception. Turner, who plays the lead character Ross Poldark, has become a sex symbol for the series, but Reed argues that such objectification would not be tolerated if the roles were reversed. She emphasizes that Turner is surprised by the level of attention and that the producers did not anticipate the public's fascination with his physical appearance. Despite fan admiration, Reed believes this obsession undermines the quality of the show.

Artist Frank McKeever has created a series of tourism posters envisioning future human habitation on various celestial bodies, including Mars, Venus, and moons of Uranus and Saturn. These imaginative images aim to inspire humanity to expand its presence beyond Earth, particularly in light of potential cosmic threats. McKeever's work reflects his passion for space exploration and retro-style design, and he believes that colonizing other planets, like Mars, could become a reality within his lifetime. The posters also highlight the challenges and opportunities associated with space travel, such as the long durations required for missions and the potential for finding life on other moons, like Europa.

Emma Louise Connolly, the girlfriend of Made In Chelsea's Ollie Proudlock, is the face of Ann Summers' new bridal lingerie line, which features soft colors and risqué designs. The collection, priced between £14 and £85, includes items like a cut-out bra and barely-there thongs, and is available in sizes 8-12 and 32A-38DD. Ann Summers, known for its erotic products, has expanded into bridal wear for the first time. Emma started dating Ollie Proudlock in October last year. The brand, founded in 1970, now has over 140 stores in the UK and sells millions of vibrators annually.

A bomb threat led to the evacuation of the Statue of Liberty on Friday, with 2,700 tourists being evacuated by boat. No explosives were found, and an official all-clear was given around 4:30 pm. The park remained closed to visitors for the day, and all evacuees received refunds. The suspicious package was discovered in a locker during a search by police sniffer dogs. The incident did not result in any injuries. Security measures include bag searches and metal detectors for all visitors, with additional screening for those wishing to enter the statue. The source of the suspicious locker remains unknown.

The Trussell Trust, a major UK foodbank network, requires churches and community groups setting up new foodbanks to pay a £1,500 donation. This fee covers staff salaries, branding, PR, and supermarket relationships. Critics argue this money could be better spent on food, especially given the organization's claim of feeding over a million people last year, which actually refers to the number of food parcels distributed. The Trust defends the fee, stating that the financial support provided to foodbanks exceeds their contributions.

Sanjay Chaddah, a property developer, was given a six-month suspended sentence for common assault and perjury after falsely accusing his neighbor's son-in-law, Dean Paton, of a racist assault during an 18-month boundary dispute. Chaddah's claims were disproven by CCTV footage showing he had knocked Mr. Paton over. The dispute began when Chaddah dug up part of the Bodens' driveway to build a wall and railings, leading to ongoing tensions between the neighbors. Chaddah's actions resulted in a suspended prison sentence rather than jail time due to the false racial accusation. The court also ordered Chaddah to perform community service and pay compensation.

PepsiCo is removing aspartame from Diet Pepsi, Caffeine-Free Diet Pepsi, and Wild Cherry Diet Pepsi in the U.S., replacing it with sucralose, due to customer concerns about aspartame's safety. This decision follows declining sales and a shift away from diet drinks associated with artificial sweeteners. Aspartame, while deemed safe by food watchdogs, has faced criticism and reports of adverse health effects, though scientific evidence supporting its safety remains strong. The change comes amid broader industry challenges, including a long-term decline in soft drink sales.

In July 2017, Craig Sytsma, a 46-year-old jogger, was fatally mauled by two cane corsos owned by Sebastiano Quagliata and Valbona Lucaj in Metamora Township, Michigan. The couple pleaded no contest to owning a dangerous dog causing death and agreed to serve 15 years in prison. The attack was the third involving the dogs since 2012, and Sytsma suffered multiple bites before being shot by a neighbor, ending the attack. The Sytsma family accepted the plea deal, though they believe current dangerous dog laws are inadequate.

West Bromwich Albion, also known as the Baggies, is being put up for sale with chairman Jeremy Peace aiming for a price between £150 million and £200 million. Due diligence is underway as interested groups from America, Australia, and the Far East show interest. Manager Tony Pulis has been assured the sale will be finalized by July to avoid disrupting pre-season preparations and summer transfers. The club recently reported pre-tax profits of £14.7 million and currently sits 13th in the Premier League. To summarize, West Bromwich Albion is on the market for £150-200 million, with due diligence ongoing and a July deadline for completion to avoid disrupting the team's operations. Photographer Jeffrey Milstein documented black boxes, revealing their appearance and importance after aircraft accidents. Despite being called "black boxes," they are typically red to aid in recovery. These devices record crucial flight data and cockpit conversations, aiding in determining the causes of crashes. Milstein gained access to air safety storage facilities in Australia to photograph various models, highlighting their evolution over time and emotional significance. His work underscores the critical role these devices play in aviation safety investigations.

Audrey Hepburn topped a poll of Britain's most stylish people, receiving 27% of the votes. David Beckham ranked third, while Victoria Beckham came in ninth. The poll, conducted for the launch of the Samsung Galaxy S6, found that women dominated the top ten, taking seven spots. Other notable figures included Princess Diana (second), Kate Middleton (fourth), and Twiggy (fifth). The survey also highlighted fashion faux pas for older adults, such as hot pants for women and ponytails for men, and suggested that people may be less likely to pursue a second date with someone deemed unfashionable. Stylish individuals were perceived as more powerful or authoritative by nearly half of those surveyed.
